name: Squares, Cubes and Roots
whatIsIt: A SQUARE of a number multiplies it by itself: 5² = 5 × 5 = 25. A CUBE multiplies it three times: 2³ = 2 × 2 × 2 = 8. A SQUARE ROOT undoes squaring: √25 = 5. A CUBE ROOT undoes cubing: ∛8 = 2. Square numbers make a perfect square of dots (25 = a 5×5 grid); cube numbers fill a cube.
whyNeeded: Squares and roots run through area (a square of side 5 has area 25), the Pythagoras theorem, algebra, and physics. Recognising perfect squares and cubes speeds up all later maths.
realLifeUses: Area of a square garden of side 6 m = 6² = 36 m²
Volume of a cube box of side 3 cm = 3³ = 27 cm³
Finding a side length back from an area (√)
Pythagoras and distances use squares and square roots

vocabulary.meaning: A number multiplied by itself three times: 3³ = 27.

story.title: The Square Tiles
story.text: Karan lays tiles in a perfect square: 6 rows of 6. 'How many tiles?' asks Bhaiya. Karan says 6² = 36. Later Bhaiya says 'I have 49 tiles — will they make a perfect square?' Karan thinks: what times itself is 49? √49 = 7. 'Yes — a 7 by 7 square!' The root gives the side back.
story.extractedProblem: 49 tiles make a perfect square. How many tiles are on each side?

visual.caption: A square number fills a perfect square grid: 5 × 5 = 25. The square ROOT of 25 is 5 (the side).

teachingGallery.title: Squares and their roots
teachingGallery.note: n² fills a square; √ gives the side back.

standardMethod.summary: To square, multiply a number by itself; to cube, by itself three times. A root reverses it — find what number gives that value.
steps: Square root: √v = the number whose square is v (√64 = 8).
Cube root: ∛v = the number whose cube is v (∛27 = 3). Learn the perfect squares to 12² and small cubes.
Ask: what number times itself is 81?

commonMistakes.fix: Squaring is multiplying by ITSELF, not by 2. 5² = 5 × 5 = 25.

commonMistakes.mistake: Guessing a square root without checking (saying √20 = 5 because 5 is near).
commonMistakes.fix: A root must square back exactly. 5² = 25 ≠ 20, so √20 isn't a whole number. Check n × n.

challenge.q: A square field has area 64 m². What is the length of one side (in m)?

questions.q: The square root of a number…

teachBackPrompt: Draw a square of dots (say 4 by 4) and show a grown-up that it equals 4² = 16, then explain that √16 = 4 gives the side back. Bonus: build a 2×2×2 cube of blocks to show 2³ = 8.

revisionCard.summary: Square: n² = n × n (5² = 25). Cube: n³ = n × n × n (2³ = 8). Square root √ undoes squaring (√25 = 5); cube root ∛ undoes cubing (∛8 = 2). Squaring is NOT ×2. Learn perfect squares 1,4,9,16,25,36,49,64,81,100.

vedicMethod.name: Vedic: squares ending in 5 (Ekadhikena Purvena)
vedicMethod.whenToUse: To square ANY number that ends in 5 — instantly, in your head.
steps: Look at the digits before the 5 (for 85, that is 8).
Multiply that by the next number up: 8 × 9 = 72.
Write 25 on the right of it.

alternateMethods.name: Vedic: squaring near a base (Yaavadunam)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To square a number close to 100 (like 98 or 103).
steps: Find how far the number is from 100 (deficit or excess): 98 is −2.
Add that step to the number: 98 + (−2) = 96 — this is the left part.
Square the step for the right part, as two digits: (−2)² = 04.
Join them: 98² = 9604. (If the square has 3 digits, carry the hundreds left.)

trickPractice.intro: Numbers that end in 5 have a magic square: multiply the front by the next number, then stick 25 on the end.

rememberIt.hook: Square = number times itself. Square root undoes it
rememberIt.unpack: 7^2 = 49, so sqrt(49) = 7. Perfect squares: 1,4,9,16,25,36,49,64,81,100. Learn these and roots become instant.

studentQuestions.a: Multiply the number by itself. 5^2 = 5 x 5 = 25. It is called 'square' because it is the area of a square of that side.
The number that was squared to get it. sqrt(36) = 6 because 6 x 6 = 36. Root undoes square.
